Case Study 01 · Polish

No more explaining your nails at the counter.

Polish is a Figma rework of The Nail Gallery, an iOS concept I originally developed. The redesign keeps the original idea, sending nail preferences to an artist before the appointment, but rebuilds the experience as a focused, four-step product flow.

View Figma prototype ↗

Role

Product & UX Design

Platform

iOS · Mobile

Tools

Figma, SwiftUI concept

Focus

Flow, hierarchy, visual system

The consultation was doing too much work.

Clients often arrive with several screenshots, uncertain terminology, and decisions still left to make. The original app supported that idea, but combined multiple choices on the same screen and relied on long explanations. The rework moves those decisions into a predictable sequence: salon, shape, length, and design.

01
SalonChoose where the request should be sent.
02
ShapeSelect almond, square, coffin, or freestyle.
03
LengthCompare options from extra short to extra long.
04
DesignUpload inspiration or let the artist decide.
✓
SentConfirm exactly what the salon received.

Before · The Nail Gallery

The original SwiftUI concept established the product idea, but the path mixed instructions, categories, and form inputs across a less predictable experience.

After · Polish

The Figma rework separates each decision, adds progress feedback, strengthens selection states, and makes the final handoff to the salon explicit.

Results

What Changed

One decision per screen

Breaking the request into four steps lowers visual load and lets each choice have a clear selected state.

Salon before customization

Starting with the destination gives every later choice context and makes the handoff feel credible.

Upload-first design step

Real clients already save inspiration images, so the rework treats that behavior as the primary path instead of forcing a preset gallery.

Visible progress

Step labels and progress lines show how much remains without adding extra navigation or explanation.

Consistent visual system

Warm neutrals, rose selection states, repeatable cards, and restrained typography create a calmer and more cohesive experience.

Clear confirmation

The final screen summarizes the information sent and gives users a path to view, update, or cancel the request.

The redesign did not add complexity. It made the original concept easier to understand by turning one crowded request into four short, deliberate decisions.

Case Study 02 · Keep Pinellas Beautiful

A live nonprofit website redesigned around how visitors take action.

My Approach

Rather than redesigning the visual brand, I focused on structural and UX problems. Each page got the same treatment: understand how people actually use it, then reorganize the content and actions around that.

01
Audit

Identify where visitors had to work too hard: buried calls to action, unclear hierarchy, content organized around internal categories instead of user intent

02
Restructure

Decide the clearest content order with the most important things first, before touching any code

03
Clarify

Add headings, labels, and spacing so pages could be understood at a glance without reading every word

04
Call to Action Placement

Move actions to the moment of decision, right next to the content that motivated them, not buried below it

05
Scalability

Build reusable sections, cards, tabs, grids, so the site could stay consistent as content grows

Events

Upcoming Events

The events page organized content around internal KPB labels, not around how a visitor looks for something to attend. The redesign changed the organizing principle entirely.

Before: grouped by internal category After: filterable calendar by date
What's wrong: Events grouped under internal headings like "Educational Presentations & Outreach." A visitor who wants to know what's happening this weekend has to read every line. No dates shown visually, no location, no way to filter.
What changed: Events now display chronologically with the date as the dominant visual element. Category tabs let visitors filter by interest. Each card shows the event type and location, everything needed to decide whether to attend, at a glance.
The shift

The old structure asked visitors to scan by what KPB calls things. The new structure lets them browse by when it's happening and what type it is, which is how people actually decide whether to go.

Donations

Donation Page

Donation pages have the highest abandonment of any page on a nonprofit site. The single biggest factor is friction at the moment of decision, and the old page created it immediately.

Before: form before context After: impact first, clear choice
What's wrong: The page opens directly into a donation form with no headline explaining why to give. The PayPal option is an awkward image block with white text overlay. Visitors are asked to choose a payment method before they've been given any reason to donate.
What changed: The redesign leads with "Help Keep Pinellas Beautiful" and a one-sentence impact statement. Payment options are restructured as two equal, clearly labeled cards. The visitor understands what they're supporting before they decide how to give.
Why this matters

Moving the impact statement above the form and restructuring payment options as a clear two-path choice directly addresses the drop-off point where most nonprofit donation pages lose people.

Results

What Changed

Every page had a different problem, but the fix was the same: reorganize the content around how people actually use it, not around how the organization thinks about itself.

Actions at the right moment

Calls to action now appear alongside the content that motivated them, next to the event calendar, above the donation form, not buried after a scroll.

Structure that matches intent

Events by date and category. Membership tiers side by side. Donation as two equal paths. Each page's structure now reflects how visitors actually make decisions on that page.

Scannable at a glance

Cards, tabs, and visual groupings replaced walls of text and raw lists. A visitor can understand each page's purpose in seconds without reading everything.
